mainEntity.name: Does QuickBooks Online have a WIP report?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. QuickBooks Online has no native work-in-progress report. Its Projects feature shows job-level income and costs, but it cannot compute percentage of completion, earned revenue, or over/under billings, and has no field for estimated total cost at completion. Contractors produce WIP schedules outside QBO: in spreadsheets, through their CPA, or with reporting tools layered on top of the QBO ledger.

mainEntity.name: How do contractors calculate over- and under-billing from QuickBooks Online data?

acceptedAnswer.text: Export costs to date and billings to date per job from QBO, collect the current estimated total cost for each job, then compute: % complete = costs to date ÷ estimated total cost; earned revenue = % complete × contract value. If billings exceed earned revenue the job is overbilled (a liability); if earned revenue exceeds billings it is underbilled (an asset). A month-end journal entry adjusts recorded revenue to earned revenue.

mainEntity.name: Is QuickBooks Online GAAP-compliant for percentage-of-completion accounting under ASC 606?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not out of the box. QBO recognizes revenue when invoices post, which is billings-basis accounting. ASC 606 requires contractors recognizing revenue over time to book earned revenue based on progress toward completion and to carry contract assets and liabilities for under- and over-billings. QBO statements can be made GAAP-compliant with monthly WIP adjusting entries, but the percentage-of-completion computation must happen outside the system.

mainEntity.name: When does a contractor outgrow QuickBooks Online for WIP and job costing?

acceptedAnswer.text: A rough threshold is eight to ten simultaneous jobs. Below that, QBO plus a disciplined monthly spreadsheet and a construction-savvy CPA is workable. Above it, or once bonding requirements, multiple legal entities, or lenders demanding monthly WIP-adjusted statements enter the picture, contractors either move to construction ERP or keep QBO as the ledger and add a reporting layer like FinBoard.ai for WIP-style reporting and multi-entity consolidation.

content:
WIP Reporting in QuickBooks Online: Why It Doesn't Exist and How Contractors Work Around It

Ask any construction CFO what report keeps them up at night, and the answer is rarely the P&L. It's the WIP schedule. That's the work-in-progress report that tells you whether each job is actually making money, whether you've billed ahead of the work or behind it, and whether the profit you booked last quarter is about to evaporate on a job that's running over budget. Your surety asks for it. Your bank asks for it. Your CPA asks for it at year-end. And QuickBooks Online, the accounting system most small and mid-sized contractors run on, simply does not produce one.

That's not an exaggeration or a configuration problem. There is no button in QuickBooks Online that generates a real WIP report. Contractors work around the gap with spreadsheets, add-ons, or a monthly ritual of exporting job cost data and rebuilding the schedule by hand. This article covers what a WIP schedule actually is, why QBO can't produce one, the accounting behind percentage of completion and over/under billing, where the spreadsheet workaround breaks down, and what a better setup looks like.

What a WIP Schedule Is and Why Sureties and Lenders Demand It

A work-in-progress schedule is a job-by-job snapshot of every open contract. For each job, it lays out the contract value, costs incurred to date, estimated total costs, percentage of completion, revenue earned to date, amounts billed to date, and the resulting over- or under-billing position. It's the bridge between your job costing and your financial statements, and the single best early-warning system for jobs going sideways.

Here's the core structure of a typical WIP schedule:

JobContract ValueCosts to DateEst. Total Cost% CompleteEarned RevenueBilled to DateOver/(Under) Billing
Riverside Office Buildout$1,000,000$400,000$800,00050%$500,000$560,000$60,000 over
Maple St. Retail TI$450,000$252,000$360,00070%$315,000$280,000($35,000) under
Hwy 12 Site Work$780,000$156,000$650,00024%$187,200$187,200$0

Why do outside parties care so much? Three reasons:

  • Sureties use the WIP schedule to underwrite your bonding capacity. Profit fade, meaning jobs whose estimated margin shrinks quarter over quarter, is the first thing a bond underwriter looks for, and it only shows up on a WIP schedule.
  • Lenders want to know whether your balance sheet is inflated by overbillings. Cash collected ahead of work performed looks like liquidity, but it's really a liability. You owe the work.
  • Your own management team needs it to catch jobs drifting over budget while there's still time to act. Industry surveys consistently find that roughly 68% of construction firms report project failures tied to inaccurate cost tracking. The WIP schedule is the discipline that forces cost estimates to confront reality every single month.

A contractor without a current WIP schedule is flying on instruments that lag reality by months.

Why QuickBooks Online Can't Produce a WIP Report

QuickBooks Online is a general-purpose small-business ledger, and it's genuinely good at that job. But a WIP report requires things QBO's data model was never designed to hold:

  • No native WIP report exists. QBO offers Projects, job-level profitability views, and budget features, but nothing that computes percentage of completion, earned revenue, or over/under billings. The words "work in progress" don't appear in its report list.
  • No home for estimated total cost. Percentage of completion under the cost-to-cost method requires a current estimate of total cost at completion for every job, a number that changes monthly as change orders land and productivity shifts. QBO has no structured field for it, so the most important input to the WIP calculation lives outside the system entirely.
  • Cost splits are painful. Sureties and project managers want costs broken out by labor, materials, equipment, and subcontractors. In QBO you can approximate this with items or account structure, but you can't easily and reliably split job costs across those categories, especially when payroll runs through a third-party service and equipment costs are allocated after the fact.
  • Budget-vs-actual per job in real time is hard. QBO's budgeting works at the company or class level on a fiscal-year grid. Job budgets don't follow the fiscal year; they follow the contract. Getting a live "budget vs. actual vs. estimate-at-completion" view per job means exporting and rebuilding it elsewhere.
  • No GAAP-compliant revenue recognition engine. QBO recognizes revenue when you invoice. Percentage-of-completion accounting under ASC 606 recognizes revenue as performance obligations are satisfied over time, which is a fundamentally different model. QBO has no mechanism to compute earned revenue, book the over/under billing adjustment, or maintain the contract asset and liability accounts that GAAP requires. (This is a broader QBO limitation; we covered the general problem in our guide to ASC 606 revenue recognition and its struggles with QuickBooks Online.)

None of this makes QBO a bad choice for every contractor. A small GC or specialty trade running fewer than eight to ten simultaneous jobs, with simple contracts and no bonding requirements, can absolutely run on QBO plus a disciplined monthly spreadsheet. The trouble starts when job count, contract complexity, or outside reporting requirements grow past what a spreadsheet ritual can keep up with.

The Accounting: Percentage of Completion and Over/Under Billing

Before we talk about workarounds, it's worth being precise about the math, because this is where spreadsheet WIP schedules most often go wrong.

The core formulas

Under the cost-to-cost method, the most common measure of progress for contractors under ASC 606, the calculation runs:

  • % complete = costs incurred to date ÷ estimated total cost at completion
  • Earned revenue = % complete × contract value
  • Overbilled when billings to date > earned revenue (a liability: billings in excess of costs and estimated earnings)
  • Underbilled when earned revenue > billings to date (an asset: costs and estimated earnings in excess of billings)

Take the Riverside job from the table above. Costs to date are $400,000 against an $800,000 estimated total cost, so the job is 50% complete. Earned revenue is 50% × $1,000,000 = $500,000. But the contractor has billed $560,000, thanks to a front-loaded schedule of values plus an early mobilization billing. That job is overbilled by $60,000. The $60,000 of cash collected is not profit; it's work owed to the owner.

The month-end journal entries

Assume invoices post to revenue as they're billed (the standard QBO behavior). At month-end, revenue on the books for Riverside is $560,000, but earned revenue is only $500,000. The adjusting entry pulls revenue back to what's actually been earned:

  • Debit Construction revenue : $60,000
  • Credit Billings in excess of costs and estimated earnings (liability) : $60,000

Now flip it. The Maple St. job is 70% complete with earned revenue of $315,000, but only $280,000 has been billed, so it's underbilled by $35,000. The entry recognizes the revenue earned but not yet invoiced:

  • Debit Costs and estimated earnings in excess of billings (asset) : $35,000
  • Credit Construction revenue : $35,000

Debits equal credits in both entries, and each reverses at the start of the next period so the schedule can be recomputed fresh. Two things follow from this mechanic. First, your income statement margin per job is only as accurate as your estimated total cost. A stale estimate silently overstates or understates profit. Second, chronic underbillings deserve as much scrutiny as overbillings: they often mean unapproved change orders, costs on the wrong job, or billing that's simply behind. All of these quietly finance the owner's project with your cash.

The Spreadsheet Workaround and Where It Breaks

The standard workaround looks like this: export job-level cost and billing data from QBO each month, paste it into a WIP template, hand-key the estimated total cost for each job (usually collected from project managers by email), let the spreadsheet compute % complete and over/under billings, then book a single adjusting journal entry back into QBO. Many excellent construction CPAs run exactly this process for their clients.

It works until it doesn't. The failure modes are predictable:

  • Stale exports. The WIP schedule reflects the moment of export. Late AP bills, unposted payroll, and pending subcontractor invoices arrive after the export and never make it into that month's percent-complete math.
  • Version drift. Two people "fix" the same workbook in parallel. The version your surety received in March doesn't tie to the version your controller updated in April, and nobody can reconstruct why.
  • Broken formulas. A row inserted for a new job doesn't inherit the earned-revenue formula. The schedule still totals; it's just wrong. Nobody notices until year-end.
  • Estimate-at-completion by email. The most judgment-heavy number in the whole schedule, estimated total cost, arrives via PM emails with no audit trail, no history, and no challenge process. Profit fade hides in exactly this number.
  • The multi-entity wall. Contractors who hold jobs, equipment, and real estate in separate LLCs, a very common structure, end up with a spreadsheet per QBO file plus a master consolidation workbook. Intercompany equipment charges and shared overhead allocations make the roll-up genuinely hard, and the consolidated WIP a lender wants becomes a quarterly archaeology project.

Each failure is small on its own. Compounded monthly across ten or twenty jobs and two or three entities, they're why so many contractors discover at year-end, when the CPA finally rebuilds the WIP properly, that the profit they thought they made during the year wasn't real.

What Good Looks Like

You don't need an ERP migration to fix this. A sound WIP process for a QBO-based contractor has five properties:

  • One source of truth for costs. Every job cost lands in QBO coded to the right job (via Projects or classes) and the right cost type: labor, materials, equipment, subs. The coding discipline gets enforced weekly, not repaired quarterly.
  • Estimates-at-completion with a heartbeat. PMs update estimated total cost on a fixed monthly cadence, in a structured place, with prior estimates preserved so profit fade is visible as a trend rather than a year-end surprise.
  • Automated math. Percent complete, earned revenue, and over/under billing computed by formula from live GL data, never hand-keyed.
  • A monthly close ritual. The over/under billing entry is booked every month with debits and credits that tie exactly to the schedule, so the interim financial statements your surety and lender see are actually GAAP-basis, not billings-basis.
  • Roll-ups that don't require heroics. Job-level, entity-level, and consolidated views produced from the same underlying data, so the numbers agree with each other by construction.

How FinBoard.ai Helps

FinBoard.ai is a reporting and consolidation layer built for exactly this kind of QBO gap. It syncs your QuickBooks Online general ledger, every transaction line with its class, project, customer, and account detail, into a governed reporting model and lets you build the reports QBO can't produce natively:

  • Custom report models for WIP-style reporting. Define earned revenue, over/under billing, and margin-to-date as formulas over your synced GL, computed the same way every month instead of rebuilt by hand.
  • Job-level rollups via classes and projects. If your job costs are coded to QBO classes or projects, FinBoard can slice cost, billing, and margin per job and by cost category (labor, materials, equipment, subs), and drill from any rollup down to the underlying transactions.
  • Consolidated multi-entity views. For contractors running multiple LLCs, say an operating company, an equipment company, and a real-estate entity, FinBoard consolidates across QBO files with intercompany eliminations, so the WIP-adjusted consolidated statement your lender wants is a report, not a project. We've written about why this is so painful to do by hand in multi-entity consolidation gaps and how to close them.
  • Numbers that reconcile. Every figure traces back to QBO source transactions, so when the surety asks how a number was built, the answer is a drill-down, not a shrug.

An honest note: FinBoard is a reporting layer, not a job-costing system. It won't capture field hours, track committed subcontract costs, or manage your estimates-at-completion. Those inputs still need to come from disciplined coding in QBO and a real process with your PMs, or from a dedicated field/job-costing tool. What FinBoard removes is the fragile spreadsheet layer between your ledger and your reports: the exports, the broken formulas, the version drift, and the multi-entity consolidation grind. Pair good field data with a governed reporting layer and the monthly WIP stops being a fire drill.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does QuickBooks Online have a WIP report?

No. QuickBooks Online has no native work-in-progress report. Its Projects feature shows job-level income and costs, but it cannot compute percentage of completion, earned revenue, or over/under billings, and it has no field for estimated total cost at completion. Contractors produce WIP schedules outside QBO: in spreadsheets, through their CPA, or with reporting tools layered on top of the QBO ledger.

How do contractors calculate over- and under-billing from QuickBooks Online data?

Export costs to date and billings to date per job from QBO, collect the current estimated total cost for each job from project managers, then compute: % complete = costs to date ÷ estimated total cost; earned revenue = % complete × contract value. If billings exceed earned revenue the job is overbilled (a liability); if earned revenue exceeds billings it's underbilled (an asset). A month-end journal entry adjusts recorded revenue to earned revenue, with the difference posted to the over/under billing accounts.

Is QuickBooks Online GAAP-compliant for percentage-of-completion accounting under ASC 606?

Not out of the box. QBO recognizes revenue when invoices post, which is billings-basis accounting. ASC 606 requires contractors recognizing revenue over time to book earned revenue based on progress toward completion and to carry contract assets and liabilities for under- and over-billings. You can make QBO statements GAAP-compliant by booking monthly WIP adjusting entries, but QBO itself performs none of that computation; the percentage-of-completion engine has to live outside the system.

When does a contractor outgrow QuickBooks Online for WIP and job costing?

A rough threshold is eight to ten simultaneous jobs. Below that, QBO plus a disciplined monthly spreadsheet and a construction-savvy CPA is workable. Above it, or once you add bonding requirements, multiple legal entities, or lenders demanding monthly WIP-adjusted statements, the manual process starts failing faster than you can patch it. At that point contractors either move to construction ERP or keep QBO as the ledger and add a reporting layer like FinBoard.ai on top for WIP-style reporting and multi-entity consolidation.

Stop Rebuilding Your WIP Schedule by Hand

QuickBooks Online will likely never ship a real WIP report. It's a general-purpose ledger, and percentage-of-completion accounting is a specialist's problem. But that doesn't mean your only options are a fragile spreadsheet or a six-figure ERP migration. Keep QBO as your system of record, keep your job coding clean, and put a reporting layer on top that computes earned revenue, over/under billings, and consolidated job margins the same way every single month. See how FinBoard.ai turns your QuickBooks Online data into the WIP and consolidated reports your surety, your lender, and your own team actually need.
